refined_fr.refined_fr: Enable measurement and reporting of material throughput in volumetric or mass units per unit time.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the system's capability to measure/report throughput metrics, and (2) the achievement of a customer-defined target. The latter is a success criterion dependent on operational execution, not a system requirement. The refined FR isolates the system's functional obligation—providing visibility into throughput—which is solution-neutral and testable independent of whether a specific productivity target is met. The target itself belongs in acceptance criteria or KPI definitions, not in the FR.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that the NGHE solution captures and reports material throughput (m³/h or ton/h) during representative operations. Verify data accuracy against independent measurement. Confirm reporting is accessible to Site Manager and Equipment Operator in real time or near-real time.
sub_frs: Acquire volumetric or mass flow data from material handling subsystems during operation.
Calculate throughput rate (m³/h or ton/h) from acquired flow data.
Display or transmit throughput metrics to authorized stakeholders in real time or near-real time.
Log historical throughput data for post-operation analysis and reporting.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire instantaneous flow rate via continuous sensing of material stream properties (mass or volume per unit time) and integrate over evaluation period to compute cumulative throughput
DP2: Measure discrete material batches (count, mass, or volume per batch) at regular intervals and extrapolate to throughput rate over evaluation period
DP3: Monitor material level change in a bounded reservoir (input hopper or output stockpile) over time and infer net throughput from level differential and material density
DP4: Combine multiple independent measurement modalities (e.g., batch counting + continuous sensing) with cross-validation logic to report throughput with confidence bounds
DP5: Establish a calibrated reference baseline (e.g., manual tally or certified third-party measurement) and report system-measured throughput as a ratio or delta relative to baseline
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1: Acquire instantaneous flow rate via continuous sensing of material stream properties (mass or volume per unit time) and integrate over evaluation period to compute cumulative throughput

potential_sources: Sensor placement and material stream geometry: continuous sensing requires stable, representative sampling point; poor placement introduces systematic bias and couples measurement accuracy to process layout

Calibration and environmental drift: instantaneous flow rate sensing is sensitive to temperature, humidity, material property variation, and sensor aging; tight coupling between sensor fidelity and reported accuracy

Integration method and time-series noise: numerical integration of noisy instantaneous data can amplify measurement error; choice of filtering/smoothing algorithm couples DP realization to signal processing module

Evaluation period definition: throughput target acceptance is period-dependent; unclear period boundaries or variable operating conditions during evaluation can decouple reported throughput from true steady-state performance
coupling_check.notes: DP1 is logically sound and solution-neutral (does not prescribe sensor type or technology). Coupling risks are primarily at the IM layer (sensor module, signal processing module, integration algorithm module) and at the FR boundary (evaluation period definition). These are manageable if interfaces and assumptions are explicit.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Material stream properties (density, particle size distribution, moisture) remain stable during evaluation period. Risk: Variation in material properties introduces systematic error in flow rate inference; mitigation requires periodic recalibration or multi-modal sensing.

Assumption: Continuous sensing modality is technically feasible and economically justified for the material type and process environment. Risk: Some materials (e.g., sticky, abrasive, or hazardous) may degrade sensor performance; alternative DPs (DP2, DP3) may be more robust.

Assumption: Evaluation period is long enough and operationally representative to average out transient effects and startup/shutdown artifacts. Risk: Short or non-representative evaluation windows yield throughput estimates that do not reflect true operational capacity; acceptance criteria must define period length and operational state.

Assumption: Instantaneous flow rate can be measured or inferred with acceptable accuracy (typically ±5–10% for industrial applications). Risk: If sensor noise or calibration drift exceeds this tolerance, reported throughput will fail acceptance criteria; sensor selection and maintenance plan are critical.

Assumption: Integration method (e.g., trapezoidal rule, running average) is appropriate for the time-series characteristics of the flow rate signal. Risk: Poor choice of integration method or inadequate filtering can introduce systematic bias; signal processing module design must be validated against representative data.

Assumption: Worksite safety plan and operational policies do not restrict sensor placement, access for maintenance, or data logging. Risk: Safety constraints may force suboptimal sensor location or limit calibration frequency; early coordination with safety and operations teams is essential.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): Enable measurement and reporting of material throughput in volumetric or mass units per unit time, with reported throughput ≥ productivity_target over an agreed evaluation period (specify duration, e.g., 8 hours of continuous operation at steady state), subject to worksite safety and operational constraints. DP should be revised to: 'DP1-revised: Acquire instantaneous flow rate via continuous sensing of material stream properties at a representative, safety-approved measurement point; apply calibrated conversion (density, cross-sectional area, or mass-per-unit-time) to infer volumetric or mass flow rate; integrate over the evaluation period using a validated numerical method (e.g., trapezoidal rule with low-pass filtering) to compute cumulative throughput; report throughput with associated uncertainty bounds and sensor calibration timestamp.' This revision makes explicit the evaluation period, safety constraint integration, and uncertainty quantification, reducing coupling and clarifying acceptance criteria.

im_boundary.name: Vehicle
im_boundary.scope: Physical and logical boundary encompassing all onboard sensing, actuation, and real-time data acquisition systems including flow rate measurement hardware, sensor integration, signal conditioning, and cumulative throughput computation

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle transmits instantaneous flow rate and cumulative throughput measurements to external systems (NGHE Solution, Teleoperation) via telemetry channel

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle receives evaluation period parameters, sensor calibration commands, and flow measurement mode directives from external tasking authority

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle uses network link for bidirectional communication of sensor data and control signals if remote sensing or distributed computation is required

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent containment: continuous sensing hardware, signal acquisition, integration logic, and cumulative computation are all vehicle-resident subsystems. Minimal diffusion: only telemetry output and tasking input cross boundary; core DP logic remains encapsulated.

Poor containment: NGHE Solution is a high-level orchestration/planning layer, not a sensing platform. Flow rate acquisition hardware and real-time sensor integration would have to diffuse into Vehicle anyway. Computation could reside here but would require continuous data streaming from Vehicle, creating tight coupling and latency sensitivity.

Very poor containment: Teleoperation is a human-machine interface and command relay layer. Sensing hardware and real-time integration must remain on Vehicle. Placing computation logic here introduces unacceptable latency and dependency on network availability for safety-critical flow measurement.

Poor containment: Safety Services is a monitoring and alert layer, not a primary sensing subsystem. Flow rate acquisition and integration are operational concerns, not safety-specific. Placing DP here would fragment sensing responsibility and create redundant data paths.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Vehicle module is the natural and optimal boundary for DP1 because it is the physical platform where the material stream is present and where sensing hardware is mounted. Continuous sensing, signal conditioning, and cumulative integration are core Vehicle operational functions. The DP exhibits high cohesion within Vehicle (all sensing and computation elements are co-located and tightly coupled by physics and real-time constraints) and minimal diffusion (only telemetry output and tasking input cross the boundary).
containment_risks: If flow rate measurement must be validated or corrected by external systems (NGHE Solution, Safety Services), a secondary validation interface may be required, introducing weak coupling.
If evaluation period or sensor calibration parameters are dynamically updated by Teleoperation or NGHE Solution, the Vehicle must implement a responsive tasking handler to avoid stale configuration.

If network latency or Vehicle-to-external communication reliability is poor, real-time flow rate telemetry may be delayed, affecting downstream decision-making in Teleoperation or Safety Services; mitigation requires onboard buffering and local integration logic (already contained in Vehicle).

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR acceptance criterion (reported productivity ≥ productivity_target over evaluation period) is directly measurable via the Vehicle's cumulative throughput computation and telemetry output. The evaluation period is a tasking input parameter, enabling repeatable measurement cycles. Worksite safety plan compliance is a policy constraint that does not conflict with the technical measurement architecture.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. The Vehicle module owns the complete measurement chain: instantaneous flow rate acquisition, signal conditioning, integration logic, and cumulative throughput computation. External systems (NGHE Solution, Teleoperation, Safety Services) consume the measurement output via telemetry but do not participate in the core sensing or integration logic. Ownership boundary is unambiguous.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP1 (continuous sensing + integration over evaluation period) is a sound and complete solution to the FR (enable measurement and reporting of throughput in volumetric or mass units per unit time). The DP specifies the logical approach (instantaneous flow rate acquisition and cumulative integration) without over-constraining the physical implementation. The DP is technology-agnostic (mass or volume sensing) and evaluation-period-agnostic, allowing flexibility in sensor selection and measurement window. No logical gaps or contradictions detected.

diffusion_risks: Secondary validation interface risk: If NGHE Solution or Safety Services must validate or correct flow rate measurements post-acquisition, a weak coupling interface is required. Mitigation: define validation as a read-only telemetry consumer; do not allow external systems to modify Vehicle-resident integration state.

Dynamic tasking responsiveness risk: If evaluation period or sensor calibration parameters are updated by Teleoperation or NGHE Solution during operation, the Vehicle tasking handler must implement non-blocking parameter updates to avoid stale configuration. Mitigation: implement atomic parameter swap with versioning; log all calibration changes for audit.

Network latency and buffering risk: If Vehicle-to-external telemetry is delayed or lossy, downstream decision-making in Teleoperation or Safety Services may operate on stale flow rate data. Mitigation: Vehicle must implement local circular buffer for instantaneous flow rate samples and cumulative throughput snapshots; external systems must timestamp all received telemetry and detect gaps.

Minimal diffusion observed: Only telemetry output (Vehicle → external) and tasking input (external → Vehicle) cross the boundary. Core DP logic (sensing, signal conditioning, integration) remains fully encapsulated within Vehicle. Diffusion score of 0.08 is acceptable.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment within Vehicle module (score 0.92). All physical sensing hardware, signal acquisition, real-time integration, and cumulative computation are vehicle-resident subsystems. The DP exhibits high cohesion: sensing and integration are tightly coupled by physics (material stream is present at Vehicle) and real-time constraints (instantaneous flow rate must be sampled continuously). Alternative allocations (NGHE Solution, Teleoperation, Safety Services) are rejected because they lack physical proximity to the material stream, introduce unacceptable latency for real-time sensing, and fragment measurement responsibility. Vehicle is the natural and optimal boundary.

recommended_revisions: Formalize the tasking input interface specification: define schema for evaluation period parameters, sensor calibration commands, and flow measurement mode directives. Include version/timestamp fields to enable Vehicle to detect stale or conflicting updates.

Specify telemetry output schema: define fields for instantaneous flow rate (with units and precision), cumulative throughput (with units and precision), evaluation period identifier, sensor status/health, and timestamp. Include error codes for sensor faults or integration failures.

Document the Vehicle's local buffering and recovery strategy: specify circular buffer size for instantaneous flow rate samples, retention period, and behavior on buffer overflow. Define how Vehicle handles network outages or telemetry transmission delays.

Add a secondary validation interface specification (optional but recommended): if NGHE Solution or Safety Services must validate measurements, define this as a read-only telemetry consumer with no feedback path to Vehicle integration logic. Validation results should be logged externally and not modify Vehicle state.

Clarify sensor calibration lifecycle: specify how calibration parameters are loaded at Vehicle startup, how they are updated via tasking, and how calibration changes are logged and audited for traceability to worksite safety plan compliance.

Define acceptance test procedure: specify how the evaluation period is initiated, how cumulative throughput is sampled and reported, and how the final measurement is compared to productivity_target. Include test conditions (material type, flow rate range, environmental factors) to ensure representative operations.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, FR-DP, DP-IM) pass with high confidence. The FR is well-defined, the DP is logically sound and technology-agnostic, and the IM allocation to Vehicle is optimal with excellent containment (0.92) and acceptable diffusion (0.08). However, the recommended_revisions identify critical interface and operational specifications that are not yet formalized at this level. Rather than stop prematurely, decompose the DP into sub-level FRs that explicitly address: (1) tasking input interface specification, (2) telemetry output schema and reliability, (3) local buffering and recovery strategy, and (4) sensor calibration lifecycle management. These sub-FRs will ensure that the Vehicle module's internal architecture is sufficiently detailed to guide implementation and testing. Safety criticality (high) reinforces the need for explicit sub-level requirements around sensor validation, calibration traceability, and fault handling.
next_level_sub_frs: FR1.1: Accept and validate tasking inputs (evaluation period, sensor calibration parameters, flow measurement mode) with version/timestamp detection to prevent stale or conflicting configuration updates.
FR1.2: Acquire instantaneous flow rate samples continuously from the material stream sensor(s) and condition the signal to remove noise and artifacts.
FR1.3: Integrate instantaneous flow rate samples over the evaluation period to compute cumulative throughput in volumetric or mass units.
FR1.4: Buffer instantaneous flow rate samples and cumulative throughput snapshots locally in a circular buffer to tolerate network latency and telemetry transmission delays.
FR1.5: Report cumulative throughput, instantaneous flow rate, sensor health status, and evaluation period metadata via telemetry output with consistent schema, precision, and timestamp.
FR1.6: Manage sensor calibration lifecycle (load at startup, update via tasking, log changes) with audit trail to ensure traceability to worksite safety plan compliance.

FR1.7: Detect and report sensor faults, integration failures, and buffer overflow conditions via error codes in telemetry output; implement graceful degradation or safe shutdown if measurement integrity is compromised.

revise.why: The current DP1 (continuous sensing + integration over evaluation period) is logically correct but lacks explicit specification of the interface contracts and fault handling strategy. Revise DP1 to include: (a) tasking input validation and versioning protocol, (b) telemetry output schema with error codes, (c) local circular buffer strategy for resilience, and (d) sensor calibration state machine. This revision will bridge the gap between the high-level DP and the detailed sub-level DPs that will emerge from the sub-FRs. The revision does not change the core logic but makes the operational boundaries and failure modes explicit.

assumptions: The material stream is physically present at the Vehicle location, enabling direct instantaneous flow rate sensing without external relay or proxy.
Sensor calibration parameters are stable over the evaluation period and do not require real-time adaptive adjustment.
The evaluation period is a discrete tasking input (not a continuous streaming parameter) and is known at the start of measurement.
Telemetry output is consumed by external systems (NGHE Solution, Teleoperation, Safety Services) but does not feed back to modify Vehicle integration logic.
Network outages or telemetry delays do not invalidate the measurement; local buffering and post-hoc reconciliation are acceptable.
Sensor faults are detectable via signal conditioning logic (e.g., out-of-range, rate-of-change anomalies) and do not require external validation.
Worksite safety plan compliance is enforced by external policy constraints and does not require Vehicle-resident safety interlocks (e.g., emergency shutdown).
open_risks: Tasking input race condition: If evaluation period or calibration parameters are updated by multiple external systems concurrently, Vehicle tasking handler must implement atomic parameter swap with conflict resolution. Mitigation: define a single authoritative tasking source or implement a priority-based merge strategy.

Sensor calibration drift: If calibration parameters are not updated frequently enough, cumulative throughput error may exceed acceptable tolerance. Mitigation: define calibration refresh interval and implement pre-measurement calibration verification.

Circular buffer overflow: If telemetry transmission is blocked for extended periods, local buffer may overflow and lose historical samples. Mitigation: define buffer size based on maximum expected network outage duration and implement overflow handling (drop oldest vs. stop sampling).

Measurement discontinuity: If evaluation period is changed mid-measurement, cumulative throughput computation may be ambiguous (e.g., should the new period reset the counter or extend the existing one?). Mitigation: define evaluation period as immutable once measurement starts; require explicit stop-and-restart for period changes.

Sensor fault masking: If signal conditioning logic is too lenient, sensor faults may go undetected and produce silently incorrect measurements. Mitigation: implement redundant fault detection (e.g., rate-of-change limits, cross-sensor validation if multiple sensors are present) and log all anomalies.

Telemetry timestamp skew: If Vehicle clock is not synchronized with external systems, telemetry timestamps may be inconsistent with external event logs. Mitigation: implement NTP or similar clock synchronization; include clock offset metadata in telemetry output.
evidence_needed: Sensor specification and calibration procedure: confirm that instantaneous flow rate sensor(s) can achieve required precision and response time for the material type and flow rate range expected at the worksite.
Signal conditioning design: specify filter cutoff frequencies, sampling rate, and anti-aliasing strategy to ensure that noise is removed without introducing phase lag or measurement bias.
Circular buffer sizing analysis: calculate buffer size based on maximum expected telemetry transmission delay, instantaneous flow rate sample rate, and acceptable data loss tolerance.
Tasking input interface specification: define schema for evaluation period, sensor calibration parameters, and flow measurement mode; include version/timestamp fields and conflict resolution rules.
Telemetry output schema specification: define fields, units, precision, and error codes; include examples of nominal and fault-condition outputs.
Sensor calibration lifecycle procedure: document how calibration parameters are loaded, updated, logged, and audited; include traceability to worksite safety plan.

Fault detection and handling procedure: specify sensor fault detection logic, error codes, and Vehicle behavior on fault (e.g., continue with degraded measurement, stop measurement, alert external systems).

Acceptance test procedure: define test conditions (material type, flow rate range, evaluation period duration, network latency scenarios) and acceptance criteria (measurement accuracy, telemetry reliability, fault detection latency).
